coredata.dc:title: Selenium and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots with highly efficient electrochemiluminescence for ultrasensitive detection of microRNA
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jie
Chen, Jie
Zhang, Yue
Liu, Linlei
Guo, YuZhuo
Yuan, Ruo
Chai, Yaqin
coredata.dc:description: In this work, selenium and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (SeN-CDs) possessing highly efficient electrochemiluminescence (ECL) and excellent biocompatibility were synthesized as a new emitter with S2O8 2− as a coreactant for constructing a biosensor to detect microRNA-221 (miRNA-221) sensitively. Notably, the SeN-CDs exhibited superior ECL performance compared with the N-doped CDs, in which selenium with excellent redox activity served as a coreaction accelerator for facilitating the electroreduction of S2O8 2− to significantly improve ECL efficiency. Furthermore, target-induced T7 exonuclease (T7 Exo)-assisted double cycle amplification strategy could convert traces of target miRNA-221 into large amounts of output DNA to capture three-dimensional (3D) nanostructures (DTN-Au NPs-DOX-Fc) loaded with large amounts of ECL signal quencher. The constructed biosensor could realize ultrasensitive detection of miRNA-221 and has a low detection limit reaching 2.3 aM, with a successful application to detect miRNA-221 in lysate of Hela and MHCC97-L cancer cell. This work explored a novel method to strengthen the ECL performance of CDs to construct an ECL biosensing platform with sensitive detecting of biomarkers and disease diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Biosensor
Carbon dots
DNA amplification strategy
Trace detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Feasibility of the designed ECL biosensor
Stability and selectivity of ECL biosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A), corresponding particle size distribution image (B), AFM image (C) and height profile (D) of the SeN-CDs.
XPS survey spectrum of SeN-CDs (A). (B) C1s spectrum, (C) N1s spectrum, and (D) Se3d spectrum of SeN-CDs.
FTIR spectrum (A), UV–vis absorption spectrum (B), FL spectrum (C) and ECL spectrum of the SeN-CDs (D).

(A) CV and (B) EIS characterization for biosensor in [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−solution (5 mM). a. unmodified GCE, b. SeN-CDs/GCE, c. Au NPs/SeN-CDs/GCE, d. Output DNA/Au NPs/SeN-CDs/GCE, e. HT/Output DNA/AuNPs/SeN-CDs/GCE, f. 3D namostructures/HT/Output DNA/AuNPs/SeN-CDs/GCE.

(A) ECL responses for different miRNA-221 concentrations from the ECL biosensor (a: 10 aM, b: 100 aM, c: 1 fM, d: 10 fM, e: 100 fM, f: 1 pM, g: 10 pM, h: 100 pM.). (B) Calibration curve of miRNA-221 determination. The ECL biosensor performance: (C) Selectivity for different targets, (D) Stability after 20 cycles of continuous scanning.

(A) Synthesis of SeN-CDs. (B) Preparation of 3D nanostructures assembled by Au NPs and DNA tetrahedron nanostructures loaded with large amounts of DOX-Fc. (C) Fabrication of ECL biosensor for detection of miRNA-221 and its ECL response. (D) Target miRNA-221-induced T7 Exo-assisted double cycle amplification strategy.

head.ce:title: Selenium and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots with highly efficient electrochemiluminescence for ultrasensitive detection of microRNA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Endocrinology, 9 th People's Hospital of Chongqing, Chongqing, 400700, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Endocrinology, 9 th People's Hospital of Chongqing, Chongqing, 400700, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, selenium and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (SeN-CDs) possessing highly efficient electrochemiluminescence (ECL) and excellent biocompatibility were synthesized as a new emitter with S2O8 2− as a coreactant for constructing a biosensor to detect microRNA-221 (miRNA-221) sensitively. Notably, the SeN-CDs exhibited superior ECL performance compared with the N-doped CDs, in which selenium with excellent redox activity served as a coreaction accelerator for facilitating the electroreduction of S2O8 2− to significantly improve ECL efficiency. Furthermore, target-induced T7 exonuclease (T7 Exo)-assisted double cycle amplification strategy could convert traces of target miRNA-221 into large amounts of output DNA to capture three-dimensional (3D) nanostructures (DTN-Au NPs-DOX-Fc) loaded with large amounts of ECL signal quencher. The constructed biosensor could realize ultrasensitive detection of miRNA-221 and has a low detection limit reaching 2.3 aM, with a successful application to detect miRNA-221 in lysate of Hela and MHCC97-L cancer cell. This work explored a novel method to strengthen the ECL performance of CDs to construct an ECL biosensing platform with sensitive detecting of biomarkers and disease diagnosis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been developed as a novel nanomaterial with great potential in applications such as optoelectronic devices, biosensing, imaging and catalysis due to their excellent optics, stable chemistry and low toxicity (Ai et al., 2021; Ci et al., 2023; Mondal et al., 2019; Tang et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022; Zou et al., 2022). However, the weak electrochemiluminescence (ECL) emission of traditional CDs limits their application in ECL. Therefore, numerous efforts have been devoted to improve the ECL of CDs through heteroatom doping (Chen et al., 2020), introduction of carbon-related dangling bonds (Wang et al., n.d.), modulation of surface oxidation (Bao et al., 2015) and so on. Among them, heteroatom doping is a widespread and effective method to enhance the ECL of CDs, which benefited from the modulation of electronic and chemical properties (Li et al., 2019; Miao et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2023). By introducing heteroatoms (such as nitrogen(Cong et al., 2022), boron(Guo et al., 2022), sulfur(Hou et al., 2022), phosphorus (Yang et al., 2022), and so on) into CDs, the electronic structure of the CDs can be changed resulting in enhanced ECL emission. Nevertheless, limited by the low active site reactivity, the ECL efficiency is still unsatisfactory. Herein, selenium and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (SeN-CDs) with excellent ECL performance was synthesized under mild conditions, in which selenium with excellent redox activity introduced new efficient redox active sites (Hua et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2019; Qu et al., 2017a) and could serve as a coreaction accelerator for accelerating the generation of abundant sulfate radicals (SO4 •–) from the coreactant (S2O8 2−) to markedly enhance the ECL efficiency (Scheme 1 A).

Recently, enzyme-assisted target recovery (EATR) has proven to be a particularly effective method of amplifying target signals (Chai et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2023). Usually, T7 endonuclease (Exo) was applied in single cycle for signal amplification, however, its low conversion rate generally results in unsatisfactory detection sensitivity of the biosensor (Liu et al., 2018; Yin et al., 2022). Therefore, in this work, the target microRNA-221 (miRNA-221)-induced T7 Exo-assisted double cycle amplification strategy was designed to convert traces of target into large amounts of output DNA to improve conversion efficiency. In addition, a large number of DNA tetrahedron nanostructures (DTN) and gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) were assembled into 3D nanostructures to accommodate a great deal of the ECL quencher, significantly quenching the ECL signal and further improving the sensitivity.

Herein, SeN-CDs was used as ECL emitter combined with T7 Exo-assisted double cycle amplification as a signal amplification to construct a “signal-off” ECL biosensor for ultrasensitive detection of miRNA-221. Initially, DTN was synthesized via four single-stranded DNAs and connected with the surface of Au NPs through Au–S bond to form 3D nanostructures (DTN-Au NPs-DOX-Fc) loading with large amounts of DOX-Fc as quencher (Scheme 1B), which could significantly quench the ECL signal to improve detection sensitivity. Furthermore, a target miRNA-221-induced T7 Exo-assisted double cycle amplification strategy was designed to convert the trace target miRNA-221 into a substantial amount of output DNA to capture 3D nanostructures served as quenching probe. As illustrated in Scheme 1D, when the trace target miRNA-221 was present, the complex of two single-stranded DNAs and magnetic beads (S1/S2-MB) captured target miRNA-221 to form a hybrid, and the above structure was digested by T7 Exo to release S1 and the target (cycle 1). Similarly, S1 could hybridize with hairpin H, and then S1 and the output DNA were released by assistance of T7 Exo (cycle 2), where S1 could trigger cycle 2 again to generate plenty of output DNA. Therefore, the constructed biosensor responded linearly to miRNA-221 within the range of 10 aM–100 pM concentrations along with a detection limit down to 2.3 aM, and with a successful application for the detection of miRNA-221 in Hela and MHCC97-L cancer cell lysates, which provided a promising path for biomarker detection and disease diagnosis.

With reference to the literature (Li et al., 2017), SeN-CDs was synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method with minor changes. First, citric acid (0.4 g) and selenocystine (0.2 g) were dispersed in 10 mL of distilled water. And then adjust the pH of the solution to alkaline by adding NaOH to promote the dissolution of selenocystine. Subsequently, the solution was warmed at 70 °C during 24 h, dialyzed and freeze-dried to obtain a black solid.

Initially, four single-stranded DNAs (A, B, C, D) of the same volume and concentration (50 μL, 8 μM) were mixed well and then heated at 95 °C for 5 min, which was then quickly coolied to 4 °C for more than 10 h to form DTN. Subsequently, 200 μL of DTN and 400 μL of prepared Au NPs were then mixed and kept on a shaker at 4 °C for 12 h to ensure that DTN was attached to Au NPs. Afterwards, 40 μL of TE buffer containing 1 M NaCl was added. Finally, 200 μL of Dox-FcCOOH solution was added and the reaction was conducted on a shaker with 4 °C during 12 h. Centrifuge the resulting mixture and wash it twice with buffer to obtain DOX-FcCOOH-DTN-Au NPs complexes, which were then suspended in 400 μL of TE buffer and stored at 4 °C in refrigerator.

Firstly, the pretreatment procedure for the magnetic beads (MBs) was as follows: 200 μL of MBs was added to PBS buffer, after mixed well, the supernatant was removed by magnetic separation, and the washing process was repeated twice. Then, the MBs was dissolved in PBS (1 mL) containing EDC (0.04 M) and NHS (0.01 M) and subsequently stirred under room temperature (1 h) for activating the carboxylic groups of MBs. Next, the MBs was washed with PBS for three times, magnetically separated and dispersed in 1 mL of TAE buffer. Secondly, well-mixed equal volume and concentration (40 μL, 2 μM) of single-stranded DNAs S1 and S2 were hybridized under 37 °C for 2 h. Afterwards, 200 μL of MBs was mixed and stirred at 4 °C overnight to obtain S1/S2-MB. Then 5 μL of hexanethiol (HT, 1 mM) was introduced and remained at room temperature to remove non-specific adsorption for 30 min. After magnetic separation, the S1/S2-MB was dispersed in 40 μL TAE buffer. Finally, 40 μL of 1 μM annealed H and 40 μL of miRNA-221 containing T7 Exo (0.6 U/μL) were added to the above S1/S2-MB solution, reacted at 25 °C for 1 h, and the inactivation of T7 Exo was achieved by a holding time of 10 min at 65 °C.

First, 10 μL of 1 mg/mL SeN-CDs was added dropwise to a mirror-like glassy carbon electrode (GCE) and dried at 37 °C for 3 h. After that, 6 μL of Au NPs was added dropwise and kept under 37 °C for 2 h. Afterwards, 10 μL of output DNA was successfully anchored to the electrode surface by Au–S bonds after being left overnight at 4 °C. Subsequently, 10 μL of HT was employed to block the electrode for the removal of non-specific adsorption. Finally, 10 μL of DOX-FcCOOH-TDN-Au NPs were added dropwise to quench the ECL signal. ECL measurements were performed in PBS (2 mL) containing S2O8 2− (30 mM) at a voltage range of 0 to −2 V, with the PMT voltage setting at 800 V and a scan rate of 300 mV/s.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was selected to directly observe the morphology of SeN-CDs and to determine the particle size. As shown in Fig. 1 A, TEM images of SeN-CDs showed spherical particles of approximately 2 nm in average diameter (Fig. 1B). Atomic force microscope (AFM) was chosen to investigate the three-dimensional morphology of SeN-CDs. AFM images (Fig. 1C) showed a height of about 3 nm, indicating a monolayer structure.

The elemental composition of SeN-CDs was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As illustrated in Fig. 2 A, the SeN-CDs was mainly composed of C, O, N and Se. In addition, the XPS spectrum of C1s exhibited three main peaks (Fig. 2B), where the one at 284.6 eV corresponded to C–C, the peak at 286.0 eV belonged to the C–Se, C–N and C–O, while the peak at 288.0 eV could be attributed to CO. In the XPS spectrum of N1s (Fig. 2C), pyridinic could be observed at 398.7 eV and the peak at 399.8 eV was attributed pyrrolic N. The spectrum of Se3d displayed two peaks around 54.3 and 55.1 eV, corresponding to Se3d5/2 and Se3d3/2 (Li et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2015).

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum (Fig. 3 A) showed the surface functional group information of SeN-CDs. An absorption bands recorded in 3430 cm−1 was caused by the N–H bond and O–H bond stretching vibration, while the 1615 cm−1 and 1405 cm−1 absorption peaks belonged to the vibration of aromatic CC double bond and C–N, respectively (Qu et al., 2017b). As displayed in Fig. 3B, there is a peak of absorption located on 280 nm in UV–vis spectrum, which matched the π-π transition of the CC double bond. In addition, the fluorescence (FL) spectrum of SeN-CDs revealed the maximum fluorescence (FL) emission peak was positioned at 408 nm and the excitation peak appeared around 310 nm (Fig. 3C). Furthermore, the ECL emission peak of SeN-CDs was found at 486 nm in Fig. 3D and was redshifted by 78 nm compared to the fluorescence emission spectrum, which could be attributed to the difference ECL surface-state emission and fluorescence intrinsic-state emission. photoluminescence (PL) is luminescence arising from direct photoexcitation of the emitting species, while ECL is that luminescence produced by a chemical reaction between species generated by electrolysis at an electrode surface (Nepomnyashchii and Bard, 2012; Zheng et al., 2009).

To explore possible reaction mechanisms, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) was chosen to detect radicals during the reaction, and the spin trapping reagent of choice was 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO). As can be seen in Fig. S5B, DMPO and S2O8 2−solutions showed characteristics signals of DMPO-OH adduct (signal strength ratio 1:2:2:1) and DMPO-SO4 adduct (signal strength ratio 1:1:1:1:1:1) (curve a), which was due to the fact that the SO4 •– could be switched to the more reactive •OH, and therefore the EPR signal of the DMPO-SO4 adduct and DMPO-OH adduct was present simultaneously. Notably, both signal intensities increased when SeN-CDs was introduced, indicating that SeN-CDs could effectively accelerate the generation of SO4 •– from S2O8 2−. Thus, the mechanism of high ECL emission from SeN-CDs in S2O8 2− is as follows:
SeN-CDs + e– → SeN-CDs•–
S2O8 2− + e– → SO4 2− + SO4 •–
SeN-CDs•– + S2O8 2− → SeN-CDs + SO4 2− + SO4 •–
SeN-CDs•– + SO4 •– → SeN-CDs* + SO4 2-
SeN-CDs* → SeN-CDs + hv

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of the designed ECL biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed after each step of modifying the electrodes in order to verify the successful construction of the ECL biosensor. As demonstrated in Fig. 4 A, compared with the unmodified electrode (curve a), the peak current was significantly reduced with modifying the SeN-CDs on the electrode, owing to the SeN-CDs hindering the electron transport (curve b). Subsequently, after modification with Au NPs, the redox peak current increased because Au NPs have good electrical conductivity. (curve c). With the negatively charged output DNA was added dropwise to the electrode, a remarkably decreasing peak current appeared due to the charge transport hindrance of the output DNA (curve d). After the nonspecific adsorption sites was blocked by HT, the redox peak current continues to decrease (curve e). Finally, hybridization of the 3D namostructures with output DNA promoted electron transport so that a rise in the redox peak current was observed (curve f) (Li et al., 2020). These results were highly consistent with those gained from EIS (Fig. 4B). The construction of the ECL biosensor was successfully validated by the above results, and the feasibility of the strategy was also demonstrated by 16% polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) (Fig. S1).

After obtaining the optimum experimental conditions (Fig. S2), the response of the ECL biosensor to different concentrations of the target miRNA-221 was recorded. As displayed in Fig. 5 A, ECL response diminished with increasing miRNA-221 concentration and showed good linearity from 10 aM to 100 pM with the linear expression I = 11400.95–1247.70 lg c and R = 0.998 (Fig. 5B), and a limit of detection down to 2.3 aM. Additionally, Table S2 shows that the proposed strategy has a higher detection sensitivity compared with existing methods for miRNA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and selectivity of ECL biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity and stability are important indicators for the evaluation of ECL biosensor. As displayed in Fig. 5C, despite the concentration of the three interferents (miRNA-21, miRNA-144, miRNA-222) were 10 times (100 pM) concentration of target miRNA-221 (10 pM), the ECL biosensor showed a comparable ECL response to the blank group for the three interferents. While for the mixture of the target and the three interferents, the ECL response decreased significantly and was approximately the same as the ECL response for the target alone. Thus, it is evident that the proposed ECL biosensor has favorable selectivity towards the target. In addition, as could be observed in Fig. 5D, the ECL response showed no significant change after continuous scanning for 20 cycles (RSD = 1.81%), demonstrating the outstanding stability of the biosensor.

The application potential of the prepared ECL biosensor was tested by applying it in real samples, lysates from Hela cells and MHCC97-L cells were selected to evaluate miRNA-221 expression. As shown in Fig. S8, the ECL intensity decreased slightly as t the gradual increase in the number of Hela cells, while there was an obvious reduction in the ECL response when the number of MHCC97-L cells gradually increased. The different decreasing trends between the two cell lysates indicated that miRNA-221 was lowly expressed in Hela cells and highly expressed in MHCC97-L cells, which was consistent with previous reports (Callegari et al., 2019; Ghosh et al., 2020; Tan et al., 2022). In addition, to validate the accuracy of the method, the same samples were assayed using a commercial kit based on a real-time quantitative reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR) (Fig. S7). The results obtained were consistent with those of the ECL biosensor, indicating that the biosensor has good potential for sensitive detection of miRNA-221.

In summary, SeN-CDs was adopted as a novel ECL emitter in combination with a target miRNA-221-induced T7 Exo-assisted double cycle amplification strategy for fabrication an ultrasensitive ECL biosensor to detect miRNA-221. Notably, compared to the nitrogen-doped CDs, the SeN-CDs exhibited superior ECL performance, which was attributed to the fact that selenium served as the coreaction accelerator promoting the electroreduction of S2O8 2−, thus markedly improving the ECL efficiency of SeN-CDs. In addition, the target miRNA-221-induced T7 Exo-assisted double cycle amplification strategy overcame the disadvantage of low conversion efficiency of single-cycle amplification and could transform the small amounts of target into abundant output DNA, which effectively improves the sensitivity of the prepared biosensor. The prepared biosensor has been applied successfully to detect miRNA-221 in real cell lysate samples successfully, providing a promising avenue for sensitive detection of biomarkers and disease diagnosis.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

Jie Liu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Jie Chen: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yue Zhang: Data curation, Investigation. Linlei Liu: Data curation, Investigation. YuZhuo Guo: Data curation, Investigation. Ruo Yuan: Modified, Funding acquisition. Yaqin Chai: Modified, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation (NNSF) of China (21974108, 22176153 and 22174113).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

Sequence information of the nucleic acids, synthesis of Au NPs and Dox-FcCOOH, characterization of Au NPs and DTN-Au NPs, the experimental procedure of PAGE, cell culture and lysis, optimization of the ECL biosensor, ECL characterization of the ECL biosensor, DPV curves and EPR spectra of SeN-CDs, calculation of LOD, ECL performance, quantitative real-time fluorescence detecting miRNA-221 in real sample, compared with other works in the detection of miRNA.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2023.115607.
